Hau Ariki Marae

Whare Tupuna Framework

Our Whare Tupuna are alive with meaning and mātauranga. The Whare Tupuna governance framework looks at your governance across eight dimensions of Mana – reflecting that governance is more than oversight and compliance – it stewards the mana of the kaupapa and the organisation.

The eight dimensions of the Whare Tupuna are set out below. Each is colour coded to its overall assessment rating. Click on each dimension to see more information about it.

TūāpapaFoundations TekotekoLeadership Pou TokomanawaCohesion Pou TūhonoSystems TāhuhuDirection MaihiPerformance Pou TuarongoFuture-proofing MahauRelationships

Te Tukanga Aromatawai  |  Process

This Aromatawai is a kaupapa-Māori governance assessment that clarifies for your board what is working, what is not, and what to do next to elevate the mana and effectiveness of your board.

It draws on hundreds of data points and perspectives – assessing not just your governance structures and processes, but also the relationships, the leadership dynamics, cultural integration and lived experience of your board and committee members.

It draws on three lenses – five board documents reviewed (the Trust Charter, Governance Manual, AGM Board Pack, year-end financial statements and the Strategic Plan), a board self-assessment survey of 6 trustees and committee members, and four 1-1 interviews – David, Mike, Pip and Prue.

We hope this visual dashboard helps your board to easily navigate these key messages to strengthen your governance.

Te Tirohanga Whānui  |  Summary

Overall, Hau Ariki Marae has an adequate governance foundation – a mature Charter, a structured Manual, real financial accounts and a clear long-horizon vision – but the board itself is at a tipping point: most of its trustees are stepping off, and the work before the AGM will decide whether this transition becomes a reset or a rupture.

Strengths
  • A strong document foundation – an exemplary Charter, a structured Governance Manual, clean year-end accounts with a clear surplus, a substantive AGM pack and a clear 25-year strategic vision.
  • The kaupapa heart is sound – board hui respect the mana of people and kaupapa (the highest-rated area), and the board checks its big decisions against the strategy.
  • Regular community connection – a weekly waiata rōpū has grown from a handful to around thirty, regular pōwhiri, Matariki and Te Wiki o te Reo events, and a strengthening relationship with kura.
  • Real delivery and funding – having upgraded with solar and batteries, fire sprinklers, water catchment, and a Community Resilience Centre role for the town.
Areas for attention
  • Governance carries an operational load on too few hands – the board is also heavily operational, and the same few volunteers carry most actions.
  • A diligent handover is not yet planned – most of the institutional knowledge sits with trustees who are leaving, and board documents are hard to find.
  • Trustee induction isn't yet running in practice – the manual promises an induction pack, but a recent trustee hasn't received one. Role clarity is the doorway to recruitment.
  • Whānau voice mainly reaches the board at AGM time – there's a wish for an ongoing two-way channel with community beyond the AGM.
Red flags
  • Succession is an absolute necessity – with three of four trustees on their way out, and the one continuing trustee newly co-opted and not yet ratified, and no clear succession plan in place.
  • No officially elected Chair – the board's leadership is being carried informally by a departing trustee.
  • The marae buildings are not insured – a single adverse event would be unrecoverable.
  • Significant income rests on one vineyard lease – this lease may not last long, and there is no forward budget to steer by.
  • Decisions are made without clear information – and sometimes agreed actions can slip between hui.

⚑ Ngā Take Matua  |  Red Flags 10▾

⚑RF #1Three of four trustees are on their way out
Pou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
The Acting Chair and Treasurer has moved out of the region, a second trustee has also relocated, and the kaumātua trustee is planning to step off – while the one continuing trustee is newly co-opted and not yet ratified. There is no succession plan in the Manual.
⚑RF #2No elected Chair – the board's leadership is being carried informally
Tekoteko – Leadership
There has been no officially elected Chair since the previous Chair stepped back. In the meantime, hui have depended on one departing trustee choosing to call them.
⚑RF #3No clear tikanga of the marae or board
Tūāpapa – Foundations
Trustees are generally not confident with confirming the tikanga of the marae or of how the board operates given the marae is not an iwi-derived marae, and many of the board members are not Māori or strong in tikanga. This perpetuates cultural confusion in the local cultural hub.
⚑RF #4Decisions are made without background information
Pou Tūhono – Systems
Trustees receive an email agenda, but background information doesn't seem to be provided to the board before meetings. It also seems that there is no record showing what sat behind decisions. Successors would find it hard to audit or learn from.
⚑RF #5Agreed actions often slip between hui
Pou Tūhono – Systems
Actions fall to the same few people and can lapse in silence – one substantive proposal around the solar array drew interest and then had no follow-up. There is no clear action tracking process capturing accountabilities and timeframes.
⚑RF #6No annual plan or visible measures for the strategy
Tāhuhu – Direction
The Manual references an annual operational plan holding each focus area's objectives and measures. That plan is not currently in place, so the AGM has been the only check-in on progress.
⚑RF #7No forward budget to steer by
Maihi – Performance
The bank statements show what happened, but not what's planned – and governance-critical lines such as insurance, payments to people and a contingency budget aren't visible. The Manual's own Annual Budget Policy commits the board to preparing and monitoring one.
⚑RF #8Most income rests on one lease that may not last
Tāhuhu – Direction
The vineyard lease underpins the marae's income, and those close to the arrangement describe it as uneconomic for the lessee and expect it may wind down. No alternative income mechanism is clear.
⚑RF #9The marae buildings are not insured
Pou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
Cover was explored and the costs seemed prohibitive, and it hasn't been addressed since. Recent fire-protection investment may change the answer – but as it stands, a single adverse event would be unrecoverable.
⚑RF #10The daily life of the marae rests on one whānau
Pou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
Pōwhiri, tikanga and the kitchen depend substantially on the kaumātua whānau living on site. There is no apparent backup to manage or maintain these functions if their circumstances change.

Kupu Tohutohu|Top Recommendations

Click a recommendation below to expand the full detail. We have also noted their relevant governance dimension and potential timeframe for them.

1
Develop a board skills matrix
Rec #4First 90 daysTekoteko – Leadership
▾
Purpose

Helps determine where skills and representation are strong, as well as gaps in capability and capacity around the board table. Also helps identify gaps created if certain board members vacate their roles.

Context

A skills gap matrix are not evident in the Manual. This would help the Board think ahead about the people and capabilities it will need, rather than filling vacancies only as they arise.

Practical ideas
  • Utilise the Skills Matrix template from Te Whare Hukahuka
  • At an upcoming Board meeting, have each trustee note the skills they bring and the people in their networks who could be future trustees.
Cost of inaction
  • The board doesn't clarify the most important skills it needs to recruit for
  • Potential applicants can't see how their skills would be of value to the board
2
Recruit and induct the next board before the AGM
⚑1Rec #12First 90 daysPou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
▾
Purpose

Secure the marae's governance continuity through the coming transition. With most of the current trustees stepping off, there should be a plan over the next three months for who might take on the board roles. This would turn a looming gap into a reset that the whole community can get behind.

Context

Three of the four current trustees have signalled they are stepping off as their circumstances allow – the Acting Chair and Treasurer has moved to Wellington, a second trustee is now contributing from Te Waipounamu, and the kaumātua trustee has indicated he will not stay on. The one continuing trustee is recently co-opted and not yet ratified.

Succession came through as the single strongest theme across all data gathered, as the most-named risk, heightened by an absent succession policy and skills matrix in the Governance Manual, and a theme every kōrero raised unprompted. The encouraging part is that some genuine names are already in the frame – with some trustees having people in mind, and at least one approach has been made.

Practical ideas
  • Agree a transition plan in the next three weeks – where the message will be shared, who will be approached, by whom, and the date each person should be inducted by.
  • Use the AGM as the reset moment – a warm, public call to whānau with a short, plain description of what the trustee role actually involves.
  • Run a simple skills-and-representation stocktake so recruitment targets the gaps rather than just the numbers. TWH can provide a template.
  • Treat Māori Land Court ratification as a parallel admin track, not a gate to block things – new people can contribute from their first hui.
Cost of inaction
  • The board's capacity and strength falls and decisions stall, just as the transition needs momentum.
  • Institutional knowledge leaves with the departing trustees rather than transferring.
  • The remaining trustees inherit the full load alone, which is exactly what makes the role unattractive to the next person.
  • The strategy, the land and the buildings carry on without real governance oversight.
3
Stand up a part-time paid administrator
Rec #10First 90 daysMaihi – Performance
▾
Purpose

Break the volunteer capacity spiral by resourcing some key administrative basics – so trustees can govern, and the mahi still gets done.

Context

Every voice in this engagement backed some form of paid part-time administration, and the board has already discussed it once without landing it. The shape it could take seems consistent – a contractor for around a day a week, sorting marae bookings, organising hui and events, document tidy-up, funding applications and follow-through. The kōrero was clear it needs to be someone who knows how to get things done and has community connections beyond the current group – not just a minute-taker.

The finances suggest the role is affordable – especially if it drives bookings income and funding applications that cover part of its own cost.

Practical ideas
  • Finalise the position description the board has already discussed and attach a number of hours to it.
  • Agree who will complete and submit the funding application for the administrator role, and by when.
  • Build "drives bookings and income" into the role so it works toward covering its own cost.
  • Prefer a local appointment with strong community connections – ideally from one of the local Māori whānau, an idea raised in the kōrero.
Cost of inaction
  • The same few volunteers keep carrying everything, and burn out.
  • Good ideas keep lapsing in silence because no one has the hours to carry them.
  • The marae's bookings and funding potential stays untapped.
4
Protect governance time – clarify the governance–operations line
Rec #11First 6 monthsMaihi – Performance
▾
Purpose

Let a small board spend its scarce hui time actually governing – while the operational mahi can still get done, visibly and sustainably.

Context

This board also takes on much of its own operations. There are strong signals that the line between governance and operational mahi has blurred – it was named from every seat, and the trustees closest to it are candid that they lean into operations because the work has to get done.

The Marae Committee exists and meets monthly – so the architecture is there. The gap is enough capacity in the Committee to get it all done, and an agreed split of what actions belong where. The paid administrator role and a Marae Committee make this practical.

Practical ideas
  • Split each board agenda into a governance section and a brief operational update.
  • Agree which operational decisions the Marae Committee simply gets on with between hui.
  • Recruit a Chair for the Marae Committee to run the monthly operational hui.
  • Revisit the split six months after the administrator starts – it should feel noticeably lighter.
Cost of inaction
  • Governance keeps getting squeezed by hands-on mahi, so the forward-looking work never starts.
  • The trustee role keeps looking like unpaid operations work, which is exactly what makes recruitment hard.
  • Oversight vs doing blur together into burnout.
5
Elect a Chair and re-establish governance leadership
⚑2Rec #2First 90 daysTekoteko – Leadership
▾
Purpose

Give the board a named leader again, so that hui happen on a rhythm, decisions carry, and the marae has a clear voice. A settled Chair makes every other recommendation easier to land.

Context

There has been no officially elected Chair since the previous Chair stepped back. The convening role has been carried informally by the Acting Chair and Treasurer as a stopgap – and he has now left the district. Trustees described the effect plainly – if no one calls a hui, it doesn't happen.

The Governance Manual describes the Chairperson role well, but the Chair's responsibilities and delegated authorities between meetings aren't set out, and the Charter sets no term limits or handover plan for the Chair role. Two separate leadership seats need filling – a Chair of the trustees, and a Chair of the marae committee to run the monthly operational hui.

Practical ideas
  • Make electing a trustee Chair an explicit AGM item – or earlier, if a candidate is ready.
  • Write short position descriptions for both Chair roles (TWH can provide templates).
  • Add a few lines to the Manual's Chairperson section on what the Chair can decide alone between meetings, with limits.
  • Agree a deputy or backup Chair, so the role never rests on one person again.
Cost of inaction
  • Hui keep depending on one volunteer's initiative – and that volunteer is soon leaving.
  • No one else holds the rhythm, so there is currently nothing to maintain the operating habits noted in the other recommendations.
  • Outside organisations that want to engage with the board struggle to find the doorway in.
6
Secure the financial future – with a forward budget and a plan beyond the lease
⚑7⚑8Rec #8First 6 monthsTāhuhu – Direction
▾
Purpose

Shift the focus from looking backwards at historical spending to working to a budget for the year ahead, and a plan for income beyond the lease.

Context

The year-end financial statements are a strength, with income well broken out, a clear surplus, and every line compared to the prior year. But no forward budget was sighted, and the Manual's own Annual Budget Policy commits the board to preparing one and monitoring it monthly. A few governance-critical lines also aren't visible in the accounts – insurance, payments to people, a contingency fund.

Meanwhile the largest income stream (the vineyard lease) is under pressure, and those close to the arrangement describe it as uneconomic for the lessee and expect it may wind down. Ideas for the next income stream already exist in the kōrero – a solar array on the back land (with iwi-energy expertise close to the board and feasibility funding windows possible), grazing, and more bookings.

Practical ideas
  • Build a one-page forward budget off the current year's actuals, adding the lines currently invisible – insurance, payments to people, a named contingency.
  • Label the purpose of each bank account so that reserves and earmarked funds are visible.
  • Task one person to scope the solar feasibility option when the next funding window opens.
  • Add 'lease dependence' into the Risk Register and agree on how this will be tracked and mitigated.
Cost of inaction
  • The financial surplus erodes quietly without a plan to protect it.
  • The phone call about ending the lease arrives before the alternative income exists.
  • Feasibility-funding windows open and close unused.
7
Create a diligent information handover and transfer process
Rec #5First 90 daysPou Tūhono – Systems
▾
Purpose

Capture what the departing trustees hold before they go, and create a "one source of truth" location for board documents to make the marae's documents findable for whoever comes next.

Context

Most of the marae's institutional knowledge currently sits with people who are leaving. The continuing trustee has not yet seen the board's documents; there are signals that trustees can't easily find what they need; and the shared drive is described from inside as messy and unintuitive, with a longer-term preference for arrangements that respect data sovereignty.

There are live project ideas at different stages – papakāinga, solar, insurance – which risk being forgotten in the change-over. The Māori Land Court record also still lists old trustees, which is worth tidying as part of the same effort.

Practical ideas
  • Hold a handover session before the AGM – noting live projects, key contacts, where things sit, passwords and access.
  • Tidy the existing shared drive rather than adopting a new system, and agree a simple folder structure.
  • Add a single Document Hub link (for example on the website) so any trustee can reach the right folder easily.
  • Start the Māori Land Court paperwork to bring the trustee record up to date.
Cost of inaction
  • New trustees re-create everything from scratch, and some of it is simply lost.
  • Decisions and commitments made in good faith disappear with the people who made them.
  • The legal record drifts further from reality just when new appointments need it accurate.
8
Make risk visible – insurance first, and a one-page risk picture
⚑9Rec #13First 90 daysPou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
▾
Purpose

Give the board a simple, regular view of the risks that it is carrying – starting with those that have the greatest consequences.

Context

The marae buildings are not insured. Cover was explored previously and priced prohibitively, and the question has sat since – but the recent investment in fire protection may have changed the pricing, and the board won't know until it asks.

More broadly, the Governance Manual already carries a complete Risk Management policy and a risk identification template – but risk isn't visible at hui, and respondents named serious, specific risks that should be on the radar: Financial, Key-person, and Health & Safety around the kitchen and the residential dwellings. Views differ across the board on how much attention these risks need – which is itself a reason to put one shared picture on the table.

Practical ideas
  • Revisit an insurance quote now that the sprinkler and fire-safety work is in.
  • Use the Manual's existing template to build a one-page Risk Register, and review it at each hui.
  • Map the basic kitchen and venue safety essentials in one working session – pairing someone who knows the buildings with someone who doesn't.
  • Keep a short Health & Safety standing item on the committee agenda.
Cost of inaction
  • A single adverse event – fire, injury, a compliance failure – could be a serious issue for the board, or unrecoverable for an uninsured marae.
  • Legal obligations around buildings and dwellings sit unowned.
  • Capable people are reluctant to step into trustee roles while the liability picture feels unresolved.
9
Build the board's operating rhythm – calendar, information, minutes, action tracking
⚑4⚑5Rec #6First 6 monthsPou Tūhono – Systems
▾
Purpose

Create a light but reliable rhythm – where papers are sent before hui, decisions are recorded and actions are tracked – so the board's limited time compounds instead of evaporates.

Context

Trustees currently receive an email agenda; background information isn't present in advance, and no strong record exists to show what information sat behind decisions. Agreed actions tend to fall on the same few people and can lapse in silence – one substantive proposal drew interest and then had no follow-through.

There is broad agreement of what is missing – an annual calendar, a document folder, an agenda circulated beforehand, strong minute-taking, and accountability for reporting back.

Practical ideas
  • Adopt a one-page standard agenda as well as decision papers and draft motions sent out before each hui.
  • Keep an action log with named owners and dates, reviewed at the top of each hui.
  • Trial an AI minute-taker to cut the admin load.
  • Run a shared calendar covering bookings, hui and key dates.
Cost of inaction
  • Decisions keep being made without records anyone can return to.
  • Actions keep sinking, and the people responsible for the follow-through and follow-up keep paying for it.
  • The transition happens without a trail of decision-making that the new board can learn from.
10
Turn the vision into a trackable annual plan
⚑6Rec #9First 6 monthsTāhuhu – Direction
▾
Purpose

Embody the mana in the strategy by giving it motion – with a brief annual plan that sets out a few measures, so the board can see progress rather than just direction.

Context

The strategy itself is a genuine strength – a clear 25-year vision, five focus areas, and a board that habitually checks big decisions against it. The Manual's strategy scaffolding is strong, and points to an annual operational plan holding each focus area's objectives and measures.

That plan is actually not in place however (confirmed in the kōrero) – so the AGM has been the only check-in on progress. A recent strategy refresh means this is not about re-writing the strategy; it is about the thin layer between vision and action.

Practical ideas
  • For each of the five focus areas, agree one or two specific 12-month targets.
  • Have a short progress note on each of the focus areas as a regular agenda item at hui, and at the AGM.
  • Consider starting this before the AGM so that you've addressed it internally before it is asked about at the AGM. Then the practice will be in place when the new board start after the AGM.
Cost of inaction
  • Effort spreads thin because nothing says which few things matter this year.
  • The annual strategic review stays a conversation rather than an assessment.
  • The vision slowly loses its grip on day-to-day mahi.

Kupu Āwhina  |  Suggestions

Additional recommendations that we identified, which sit alongside the Top Ten. Click each one below to expand the full detail.

11
Turn connection into a channel – whānau voice and the next generation
Rec #15First 6 monthsMahau – Relationships
▾
Purpose

Convert the marae's genuine community standing into a source of the two things that governance needs from it – a steady two-way channel with whānau, and a pipeline of contributors.

Context

The connection engine is real – including the weekly waiata rōpū that has grown from a handful to around thirty, regular pōwhiri, strong Matariki and Te Wiki o te Reo events, and a working relationship with the kura. But there are consistent signals that whānau input to Marae Committee activity and into board decisions is thin.

The bridge has not yet been built – so people attend, but there is no transition process from attending to contributing and eventually governing. The 'missing middle' (30 to 45 year olds) are busy and haven't been asked in ways that fit their lives, and the young people who do come tend to want real mahi to do, not sit in meetings.

Practical ideas
  • Start a regular short pānui on what the board is working on and needing.
  • Add a standing 'Succession' hui item – who are we drawing in, and toward what role?
  • Offer defined, hands-on jobs (working bees, events, projects) as a first contribution mechanism, rather than committee seats.
  • Explore the regional governance-activation idea alongside other marae – building a shared pool of future trustees across the local marae.
Cost of inaction
  • Whānau engagement keeps producing attendance, not internal capacity.
  • The next recruitment round starts cold.
  • Whānau voice stays an annual snapshot only, and decisions lose the legitimacy that voice brings.
12
Run the induction that the Manual promises – and build trustee capability
⚑3Rec #3First 6 monthsTekoteko – Leadership
▾
Purpose

Ensure that joining the board feels clear and supported from day one. The Manual already promises it – the practice has to catch up.

Context

The Governance Manual's induction section is complete on paper – an induction pack with the Charter, key policies and what a new trustee needs. In practice, the newest trustee has received none of it and could not describe what the role expects of them – at exactly the moment the board most needs new people to say yes.

The kōrero also named the deeper opportunity: trustees upskilling in both governance and te ao Māori as part of the value that the role offers – a reason to join, and an answer to those who are quietly anxious that they don't know enough.

Practical ideas
  • Assemble the induction pack that the Manual describes, and hand it to every incoming trustee before the AGM.
  • Pair each new trustee with a current one for a short orientation walk-through.
  • Build a cultural-and-governance learning element into the trustee experience – a drawcard, not homework.
Cost of inaction
  • The roles stay unclear and good people keep hesitating.
  • New trustees start cold in their first months.
  • The capability gap remains over time.
13
Plan for 'manaakitanga continuity' around the on-site kaumātua whānau
⚑10Rec #14First 6 monthsPou Tuarongo – Future-proofing
▾
Purpose

Protect the welcoming heart of the marae by making the on-site role visible, supported and survivable.

Context

The day-to-day life of the marae – pōwhiri, tikanga, the kitchen and a constant presence – rests substantially on the kaumātua whānau living on site. Their presence is described as transformative: the marae feels like it is living. It was also raised named as a clear key-person risk – if they were no longer living at the marae, there is no documented backup option, and the pōwhiri function would become strained.

There are also signals that they are imposing and carrying tikanga standards that may not be shared by the board and community, without an agreed process behind them. This can place them at the centre of community friction that the board really needs to navigate.

Practical ideas
  • Hold an open kōrero with the whānau – what the 'manaakitanga' roles require, what support they need, what respite looks like.
  • Document the manaakitanga essentials (openings, bookings, kitchen, incidents) so others can step in when needed.
  • Agree a board-owned process for handling incidents on the marae, so no individual carries that alone.
  • Build a small support roster of regular attendees for the practical mahi.
Cost of inaction
  • A single change of circumstance could quickly hollow out the marae's daily life and feel.
  • Tikanga friction lands on a person instead of a process.
  • The manaakitanga knowledge never transfers to the next generation.
14
Lift the board packs to support decision-making
⚑4Rec #7First 6 monthsPou Tūhono – Systems
▾
Purpose

Help trustees arrive ready to make the necessary governance decisions. The meeting discipline is already there – the decision-support layer is the next step.

Context

The AGM pack that we reviewed is genuinely strong – with a timed agenda, a substantive board report, the prior minutes, a clean and consistent layout. Ideally this is what the board receives prior to every hui. One key layer that is missing is the decision-support layer: financial updates included, items labelled as 'Decision' or 'Information', short background papers for the bigger calls, and pre-drafted resolution statements.

Practical ideas
  • Add a 'Decision' / 'Information' label beside each agenda item.
  • Attach a financial update to the board pack that goes out beforehand.
  • Write a half-page background paper for the single biggest decision each time, and build the habit from there.
  • For each board decision, ensure a pre-drafted resolution statement that the board can mull over.
  • Consider the template meeting agenda and board pack checklist from Te Whare Hukahuka
Cost of inaction
  • Decisions keep being made cold at each meeting.
  • The audit trail stays thin, which is made even riskier given current decision-makers stepping away.
  • Board members can't properly prepare, so participation stays shallower than it could be.
15
Ensure the Charter accounts for trustee protection, and set a review cycle
Rec #1Next 12 monthsTūāpapa – Foundations
▾
Purpose

Round out an exceptionally strong Charter with protections that trustees deserve, and a rhythm that keeps it current.

Context

The Charter review found almost everything was in place. There are several areas that could be added or strengthened, including: a trustee liability and indemnity provision; a remuneration and reimbursement clause (to enable the Manual's expenditure policy to be enacted); decision thresholds, substantial-transaction levels, and Chair term limits.

Having a five-yearly review cycle of the Charter would also allow for additional improvements over time to be caught and made.

Practical ideas
  • Table a template indemnity clause (TWH can provide one) and agree how trustees acting in good faith are protected.
  • Set a first Charter read-through date, with a five-yearly cycle thereafter.
  • Finalise the Manual's current placeholder dates and bracketed text so Version 1.0 can be formally adopted.
Cost of inaction
  • Trustees carry personal uncertainty that the Charter could remove – which could be creating resistance in recruitment.
  • The identified gaps remain or accumulate, instead of being swept up on a cycle.
  • The Manual stays in draft permanently.
Tūāpapa | Foundations An exceptionally strong Charter and a lived tikanga base – with just a few the work procedural ahead is two small protections and keeping the record current.
▾

Tūāpapa represent the foundations that guide your board's decisions and behaviour – including the kaupapa, values, tikanga, policies and core documents that anchor everything above. They are the load-bearing beliefs that remain over time and shape how your board operates into the future.

Reflects Mana Whenua – mana built from drawing on tūpuna-led guidance that serves the present and will outlast any one board to safeguard the future.

“Whatungarongaro te tangata, toitū te whenua”
People pass on, but the land remains.
Western lens
Foundations are documents to be authored, ratified and audited.
Kaupapa Māori lens
Foundations are living tikanga that shape how decisions are made and how the board carries the kaupapa between hui and over time.
The foundations are the marae's strongest governance asset.

Almost every core element of a founding document is in place – with just a few gaps around protections and housekeeping.

Strengths
A founding Charter with almost everything in place▾
The Charter clearly sets out 24 core elements – purpose, membership, trustee appointment, meetings, quorum, voting, duties and powers, conflicts of interest, financial procedures, AGM, delegation and amendment – including a tikanga-based dispute resolution process and an explicit intergenerational focus.
Tikanga is evident, even if not fully written▾
As a Ngā Hau e Whā community marae, Hau Ariki is holding its own tikanga in practice at it's hui – including karakia, regular pōwhiri, and a clear shared sense of why the marae was built in the first place.
Conflicts of interest are being handled▾
A complete process is set out in both the Charter and the Manual – including disclosure, interests register tabled, and stepping aside from discussion and voting.
Areas for attention
Two protections are not yet in the Charter▾
A trustee liability and indemnity provision, and a remuneration and reimbursement clause, are not evident – noting that reimbursement is covered in practice by the Manual's expenditure policy.
No set review cycle for the founding document▾
A five-yearly read-through would keep the Charter matched to how Hau Ariki actually operates, and absorb the smaller refinements over time.
The Māori Land Court record needs tidying▾
The trust record still lists old trustees. With new appointments coming, bringing the record up to date will matter more than usual.
⚑RF #3
No clear tikanga of the marae or boardTrustees are generally not confident with confirming the tikanga of the marae or of how the board operates given the marae is not an iwi-derived marae, and many of the board members are not Māori or strong in tikanga. This perpetuates cultural confusion in the local cultural hub.

Recommendations to improve your Tūāpapa

Ensure the Charter accounts for trustee protection, and set a review cycle
Rec #1Next 12 months
▾
Purpose

Round out an exceptionally strong Charter with protections that trustees deserve, and a rhythm that keeps it current.

Context

The Charter review found almost everything was in place. There are several areas that could be added or strengthened, including: a trustee liability and indemnity provision; a remuneration and reimbursement clause (to enable the Manual's expenditure policy to be enacted); decision thresholds, substantial-transaction levels, and Chair term limits.

Having a five-yearly review cycle of the Charter would also allow for additional improvements over time to be caught and made.

Practical ideas
  • Table a template indemnity clause (TWH can provide one) and agree how trustees acting in good faith are protected.
  • Set a first Charter read-through date, with a five-yearly cycle thereafter.
  • Finalise the Manual's current placeholder dates and bracketed text so Version 1.0 can be formally adopted.
Cost of inaction
  • Trustees carry personal uncertainty that the Charter could remove – which could be creating resistance in recruitment.
  • The identified gaps remain or accumulate, instead of being swept up on a cycle.
  • The Manual stays in draft permanently.
Board Documents – Tūāpapa▾
Trust Charter & Land Information Pack – 🟢 An exceptionally strong founding document
2472
▾
Together, the Hau Ariki Marae Charter and accompanying land information pack give Hau Ariki a strong, culturally grounded governance foundation. The Charter sets out clear trustee roles, meeting rules, and a tikanga-based way to resolve disputes. The land pack records where the reservation came from and its legal status.
24 items complete – Almost every core element of a founding document is clearly set out – purpose, membership, trustees, meetings, conflicts of interest, financial procedures, delegation, amendment, tikanga framing and dispute resolution.
7 items partial – Several governance mechanics are referred to or touched on, but not fully specified.
2 gaps – Two contemporary protections often expected in a founding document are not evident.
The Charter clearly sets out
Charitable purposes / kaupapa
Background and origin of the Trust
Geographic scope of the Trust
Clarity of beneficial members
Number of trustees (min/max)
Trustee appointment processes & terms
Appointment of Chair & other key positions
Meeting procedures
Voting & resolutions
Quorum definition
Trustees' duties
Trustees' powers
Conflicts of interest process
Financial procedures
AGM requirements
Sub-committees (establishment & use)
Powers to delegate
Governance vs operations clarity
Charter amendment process
Engagement with affiliated groups & named stakeholders
Te Ao Māori / tikanga framing
Tikanga-based dispute resolution
Collective decision-making recognised
Intergenerational / legacy focus
protections TO ADD
Trustee liability & indemnity
Remuneration & reimbursement provision
Touched on, but not fully specified
Definitions for key terms
Decision-making thresholds (majority vs special vs unanimous)
Independent Board Secretary▾Reasoning on the close callIndependent Board Secretary – amber. The Secretary role is well defined, but it sits with a trustee rather than an independent person. The Charter gives the Secretary clear duties – calling meetings, keeping minutes, sending notices, holding records. The Marae Committee's Third Schedule does allow non-trustee officers, which is the closest the documents come.
Board performance review (removal is covered)▾Reasoning on the close callBoard performance review (removal is covered) – amber. The Charter covers removing a trustee but not reviewing the board's own performance. It sets out a clear process to remove a trustee through a Special General Meeting, and the Māori Land Court if needed, and absence from three meetings in a row triggers that process. What's not there is any regular check on how the board itself is doing – for example, an annual board self-review.
Chair succession & term limits▾Reasoning on the close callChair succession & term limits – amber. The Charter rotates trustees but sets no limit on how long someone can serve as Chair. Trustees hold office for three years, with a rotation so no more than a third change in any one year, and the trustees choose the Chair after each AGM. But retiring trustees can stand again with no cap, and there's no plan for handover if the Chair leaves between AGMs.
Substantial transactions thresholds & processes▾Reasoning on the close callSubstantial transactions thresholds & processes – amber. There are some financial controls but no set threshold for big decisions. The Charter requires two signatories on bank accounts and trustee-ratified financial policies, and the land pack notes the reservation land can't be sold and can only be leased with Māori Land Court direction. What's not set is an internal dollar level above which a transaction must come to the full board.
Register of members / beneficiaries
Recommendations
Trustee protection and expenses

Your board should decide and write down two things – how trustees are protected when they act in good faith, and what types of costs a trustee can claim back. This gives trustees confidence and keeps spending clear. To get this started: TWH can provide a template. Table it at a board hui and agree what reasonable, pre-approved expenses look like for your trustees.

Decision-making thresholds

Your board should agree which decisions need a simple majority, which need a higher level of agreement, and which need everyone to agree – and write this down. To get started: List your most common decision types and agree at a hui what level of agreement each one needs.

Board renewal and review

Your board should set how long someone can serve as Chair, plan for a handover before a Chair steps down, and review board performance once a year. The Charter already covers removing a trustee – this adds the forward-looking side. One way to begin: Add a short board self-review to your annual cycle, and agree a simple plan for who steps up if the Chair leaves mid-term.

Big financial decisions

Your board should set clear thresholds for major financial decisions – the dollar level above which a decision must come to the full board, and how it gets recorded. A first step: Agree one or two dollar thresholds that always come to the full board.

Charter review cycle

Formally review the Charter on a set cycle – at least every five years – to check it still matches how Hau Ariki actually operates. One way to begin: Nominate one or two trustees to lead a review and set a date for a first read-through.

Tekoteko | Leadership Willing, capable people and well-described officer roles on paper – the Chair seat itself is the gap.
▾

The Tekoteko is the carved figurehead that represents the leadership at the peak of the whare. In governance, it includes having solid governance roles, collective responsibility, board composition and oversight of organisational functions. Rather than focusing on status and control, strong governance is about enabling those that you represent so they can thrive and keep your kaupapa alive.

Reflects Mana Tuku Iho – mana vested in the people who lead that compels us to act in ways that honour those before us, for the benefit of the collective.

“Mā mua ka kite a muri, mā muri ka ora a mua.”
Those who lead give sight to those who follow; those who follow give life to those who lead.
Western lens
Leadership is held by discrete role-holders bound by position descriptions and delegation.
Kaupapa Māori lens
Leadership is relational and shared – Chair, kaumātua and the wider board all carrying mana for different parts of the kaupapa.
Leadership is being carried informally, and the carrier is leaving.

The gap is structural, not personal – the board has named these itself, and has candidates in mind.

Strengths
Officer roles are well described in the Manual▾
The Chairperson, Board Secretary, Treasurer and Marae Committee Chair are defined – a strong base to appoint into.
Leadership instincts are intact▾
The kōrero consistently identified what good looks like – having a Chair of the trustees, a separate Marae Committee Chair, and a tikanga voice at the table.
Shoulder-tapping has begun▾
The succession instinct exists – trustees have some names in mind, and at least one approach has been made.
Areas for attention
Role documentation is scattered, and is thin for the Chair's authorities▾
An individual trustee's duties are spread across several Manual sections rather than consolidated into one position description, and there is no statement of what the Chair can decide alone between meetings.
Trustee capability-building isn't yet part of the role▾
There is no skills matrix, and several interviews named 'upskilling in governance' and 'te ao Māori' as both a real gap and a reason people would join.
⚑RF #2
No elected Chair – the board's leadership is being carried informallyThere has been no officially elected Chair since the previous Chair stepped back. In the meantime, hui have depended on one departing trustee choosing to call them.

Recommendations to improve your Tekoteko

Elect a Chair and re-establish governance leadership
⚑2Rec #2First 90 days
▾
Purpose

Give the board a named leader again, so that hui happen on a rhythm, decisions carry, and the marae has a clear voice. A settled Chair makes every other recommendation easier to land.

Context

There has been no officially elected Chair since the previous Chair stepped back. The convening role has been carried informally by the Acting Chair and Treasurer as a stopgap – and he has now left the district. Trustees described the effect plainly – if no one calls a hui, it doesn't happen.

The Governance Manual describes the Chairperson role well, but the Chair's responsibilities and delegated authorities between meetings aren't set out, and the Charter sets no term limits or handover plan for the Chair role. Two separate leadership seats need filling – a Chair of the trustees, and a Chair of the marae committee to run the monthly operational hui.

Practical ideas
  • Make electing a trustee Chair an explicit AGM item – or earlier, if a candidate is ready.
  • Write short position descriptions for both Chair roles (TWH can provide templates).
  • Add a few lines to the Manual's Chairperson section on what the Chair can decide alone between meetings, with limits.
  • Agree a deputy or backup Chair, so the role never rests on one person again.
Cost of inaction
  • Hui keep depending on one volunteer's initiative – and that volunteer is soon leaving.
  • No one else holds the rhythm, so there is currently nothing to maintain the operating habits noted in the other recommendations.
  • Outside organisations that want to engage with the board struggle to find the doorway in.
Run the induction that the Manual promises – and build trustee capability
⚑3Rec #3First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Ensure that joining the board feels clear and supported from day one. The Manual already promises it – the practice has to catch up.

Context

The Governance Manual's induction section is complete on paper – an induction pack with the Charter, key policies and what a new trustee needs. In practice, the newest trustee has received none of it and could not describe what the role expects of them – at exactly the moment the board most needs new people to say yes.

The kōrero also named the deeper opportunity: trustees upskilling in both governance and te ao Māori as part of the value that the role offers – a reason to join, and an answer to those who are quietly anxious that they don't know enough.

Practical ideas
  • Assemble the induction pack that the Manual describes, and hand it to every incoming trustee before the AGM.
  • Pair each new trustee with a current one for a short orientation walk-through.
  • Build a cultural-and-governance learning element into the trustee experience – a drawcard, not homework.
Cost of inaction
  • The roles stay unclear and good people keep hesitating.
  • New trustees start cold in their first months.
  • The capability gap remains over time.
Develop a board skills matrix
Rec #4First 90 days
▾
Purpose

Helps determine where skills and representation are strong, as well as gaps in capability and capacity around the board table. Also helps identify gaps created if certain board members vacate their roles.

Context

A skills gap matrix are not evident in the Manual. This would help the Board think ahead about the people and capabilities it will need, rather than filling vacancies only as they arise.

Practical ideas
  • Utilise the Skills Matrix template from Te Whare Hukahuka
  • At an upcoming Board meeting, have each trustee note the skills they bring and the people in their networks who could be future trustees.
Cost of inaction
  • The board doesn't clarify the most important skills it needs to recruit for
  • Potential applicants can't see how their skills would be of value to the board
Pou Tokomanawa | Cohesion The board room itself is healthy – mana is respected, disagreement is handled, and goodwill is real.
▾

The Pou Tokomanawa is the central support pillar of the whare – representing how well your board functions together as a collective. This includes how decisions are made, how disagreement is held, and how trust and cohesion is built and maintained.

Reflects Mana Tangata – recognising the inherent mana of individuals and the collective. This mana moves people and kaupapa forward, with each shared decision compounding to generate momentum.

“Hoea ngātahitia ki ko atu.”
Paddling together takes us all further.
Cohesion is a genuine strength to protect through the transition.

The highest-rated area across the data, confirmed by the kōrero – the risk to it is distance and turnover, not conflict.

Strengths
Board hui respect the mana of people and kaupapa▾
The highest-rated area in the assessment, and nothing in the kōrero contradicted it – karakia to open and close, and no live disputes surfaced anywhere in the engagement.
Disagreement is managed well▾
Signals consistently noted that the way the board handles disagreement as one of its strongest areas – a healthy base that most boards yearn for.
Goodwill toward the marae runs deep▾
Every voice spoke with warmth and pride about the place, its story, and each other's contribution.
Areas for attention
Physical distance thins the togetherness▾
Board members now live across three regions and hui have moved mostly online. Trustees themselves note that in a marae, people need to be together – worth designing for as the new board forms.
This is a strength area – no red flags. The focus here is on sustaining and sharpening what is already working.
Pou Tūhono | Systems Meeting discipline has some bones – board packs, records and follow-through are the systems to build.
▾

Pou Tūhono represent the systems and structures that support good governance – the architecture, the cadence, the data, and the infrastructure that allow the board to govern reliably. Strong Pou Tūhono help ensure people don't burn out and hold the kaupapa steady over time.

Reflects Mana Tōpū – mana generated from turning collective intent into clear structure and processes that carry the mauri of the kaupapa and those within it.

“Mā tini, mā mano, ka rapa te whai.”
Many hands make light work. A great number working together will achieve what a few cannot.
The board is deciding and acting in good faith, without the systems that would let anyone see it.

Each gap here is small and free to fix – together these gaps show why effort is evaporating.

Strengths
A real AGM pack exists▾
A timed agenda, a substantive board report and the previous minutes, in a clean and consistent layout – a genuine discipline to build on for board meetings.
Information flows person-to-person reliably today▾
The regular bookings summary that goes out to the Marae Committee works well and is appreciated – it just needs systematising beyond the current doer.
Areas for attention
No single home for the board's documents▾
The shared drive is described as messy and unintuitive, and there are signals that trustees can't reliably find what they need. There is also a stated preference for arrangements that respect data sovereignty.
⚑RF #4
Decisions are made without background informationTrustees receive an email agenda, but background information doesn't seem to be provided to the board before meetings. It also seems that there is no record showing what sat behind decisions. Successors would find it hard to audit or learn from.
⚑RF #5
Agreed actions often slip between huiActions fall to the same few people and can lapse in silence – one substantive proposal around the solar array drew interest and then had no follow-up. There is no clear action tracking process capturing accountabilities and timeframes.

Recommendations to improve your Pou Tūhono

Create a diligent information handover and transfer process
Rec #5First 90 days
▾
Purpose

Capture what the departing trustees hold before they go, and create a "one source of truth" location for board documents to make the marae's documents findable for whoever comes next.

Context

Most of the marae's institutional knowledge currently sits with people who are leaving. The continuing trustee has not yet seen the board's documents; there are signals that trustees can't easily find what they need; and the shared drive is described from inside as messy and unintuitive, with a longer-term preference for arrangements that respect data sovereignty.

There are live project ideas at different stages – papakāinga, solar, insurance – which risk being forgotten in the change-over. The Māori Land Court record also still lists old trustees, which is worth tidying as part of the same effort.

Practical ideas
  • Hold a handover session before the AGM – noting live projects, key contacts, where things sit, passwords and access.
  • Tidy the existing shared drive rather than adopting a new system, and agree a simple folder structure.
  • Add a single Document Hub link (for example on the website) so any trustee can reach the right folder easily.
  • Start the Māori Land Court paperwork to bring the trustee record up to date.
Cost of inaction
  • New trustees re-create everything from scratch, and some of it is simply lost.
  • Decisions and commitments made in good faith disappear with the people who made them.
  • The legal record drifts further from reality just when new appointments need it accurate.
Build the board's operating rhythm – calendar, information, minutes, action tracking
⚑4⚑5Rec #6First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Create a light but reliable rhythm – where papers are sent before hui, decisions are recorded and actions are tracked – so the board's limited time compounds instead of evaporates.

Context

Trustees currently receive an email agenda; background information isn't present in advance, and no strong record exists to show what information sat behind decisions. Agreed actions tend to fall on the same few people and can lapse in silence – one substantive proposal drew interest and then had no follow-through.

There is broad agreement of what is missing – an annual calendar, a document folder, an agenda circulated beforehand, strong minute-taking, and accountability for reporting back.

Practical ideas
  • Adopt a one-page standard agenda as well as decision papers and draft motions sent out before each hui.
  • Keep an action log with named owners and dates, reviewed at the top of each hui.
  • Trial an AI minute-taker to cut the admin load.
  • Run a shared calendar covering bookings, hui and key dates.
Cost of inaction
  • Decisions keep being made without records anyone can return to.
  • Actions keep sinking, and the people responsible for the follow-through and follow-up keep paying for it.
  • The transition happens without a trail of decision-making that the new board can learn from.
Lift the board packs to support decision-making
⚑4Rec #7First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Help trustees arrive ready to make the necessary governance decisions. The meeting discipline is already there – the decision-support layer is the next step.

Context

The AGM pack that we reviewed is genuinely strong – with a timed agenda, a substantive board report, the prior minutes, a clean and consistent layout. Ideally this is what the board receives prior to every hui. One key layer that is missing is the decision-support layer: financial updates included, items labelled as 'Decision' or 'Information', short background papers for the bigger calls, and pre-drafted resolution statements.

Practical ideas
  • Add a 'Decision' / 'Information' label beside each agenda item.
  • Attach a financial update to the board pack that goes out beforehand.
  • Write a half-page background paper for the single biggest decision each time, and build the habit from there.
  • For each board decision, ensure a pre-drafted resolution statement that the board can mull over.
  • Consider the template meeting agenda and board pack checklist from Te Whare Hukahuka
Cost of inaction
  • Decisions keep being made cold at each meeting.
  • The audit trail stays thin, which is made even riskier given current decision-makers stepping away.
  • Board members can't properly prepare, so participation stays shallower than it could be.
Board Documents – Pou Tūhono▾
Governance Manual (Te Kaupapa Here me Ngā Tikanga, V1.0) – 🟠 Strong where it matters; continuity is the gap
1052
▾
A well-organised manual that brings the board's statement of practice together with operational, marae hire, and financial policies in one document. It is strong on financial governance, risk, conflicts of interest, and the roles of key officers – the Chair, Secretary, Treasurer, and Marae Committee Chair are all clearly described, and the delegated financial authorities are set out in detail. The main opportunities sit in continuity areas: succession planning and a skills assessment are not yet in the Manual, and a standalone Health & Safety policy would complete the compliance picture. As a Version 1.0 draft, some sections still carry placeholder dates and bracketed text to be finalised.
10 items complete – Risk, conflicts of interest, financial management, expense reimbursement, officer role descriptions, delegated authorities, performance review, induction, and strategic planning are all well covered.
5 items partial – Health and safety, the Chair's delegated authorities, the board member position description, committee terms of reference, and media and communications are partially addressed but not yet consolidated.
2 gaps – A succession planning process and a skills gap matrix are not evident in the Manual.
FOR COMPLIANCE
Health & Safety policy
Risk Management policy
Conflicts of Interest policy
Financial Management policy
Reimbursement of expenses policy
FOR ROLE CLARITY
Chairperson position description
Chairperson delegated authorities
Board member position description
Board Secretary position description
Manager / Marae Committee Chair delegated authorities
Terms of Reference for board sub-committees
Media Statements & Communications policy
FOR CONTINUITY
Board performance assessment policy
Skills gap matrix process / template
Succession planning policy
Board member induction process policy
Strategic Direction & Planning policy
Recommendations
Health & Safety policy

The Manual should include a standalone Health & Safety policy, or a clear reference to one held separately. There are Health & Safety duties currently spread across several sections (incident, security, and risk), and a dedicated policy would bring the Board's obligations into one place. To get this started: Pull the existing Health & Safety references together under one heading, setting out the Board's H&S responsibilities, how incidents are reported, and who oversees H&S risk.

Succession planning and a skills matrix

The Manual should add a succession planning process and a simple skills assessment. Together these help the Board think ahead about the people and capabilities it will need, rather than filling vacancies only as they arise. A practical first step: Utilise TWH's board skills matrix template

Role expectations – board members and the Chair

The duties of individual trustees are spread across several sections, and the Chair's specific authorities are not defined. Consolidating each into its own description would give both roles formal standing. A practical first step: Create a short Board Member position description that gathers the existing trustee expectations into one place, and add a few lines to the Chairperson section listing what the Chair can decide independently between meetings, with any limits.

Media and communications, and committee terms of reference

A dedicated communications policy would clarify who speaks on behalf of the marae and how media queries are handled. When standing committees are next established, formal Terms of Reference for each would complete the committee framework the Manual already describes. To get this started: Add a short communications policy naming the spokesperson and the approval process for public statements. Hold the committee Terms of Reference until a standing committee is formed.

AGM Board Pack (9 August 2025) – 🟠 Strong meeting discipline; thin decision-support layer
4511
▾
This is a substantial AGM pack – much more than just providing a one-page agenda. It brings together a timed agenda, a substantive board report, and the minutes of the last AGM. Together they give attendees a clear picture of the year and a clear running order for the day. What's not visible is the decision-support layer: the financial statements are listed on the agenda but not included here, and there's no Risk Register, policy review schedule, or decision papers. This ideally is what the board gets to see at its meetings.
4 items complete – Previous minutes included, a timed agenda, a clear and consistent layout, and a substantive board report.
5 items partial – The agenda and report touch on strategy and signal decisions, but don't structure them for decision-making.
11 gaps – Financial statements, risk and policy monitoring, and decision-support tools are not visible in this pack.
Strategic matters
Reports on progress against the year's mahi
Agenda items connect to strategic priorities
Agenda flow and structure is Chair-led
Decisions required clearly noted
Background info supports decision-making
Summary of SMART goals on first page
Background papers, options, and recommendations
Pre-drafted resolution statements
Administrative & compliance matters
Minutes from last meeting included
Agenda items have allocated times
Financial information (budgets vs actuals)
Risk register
Board policy review schedule
Agenda links or references to guiding policies
Use of decision-support tools
Dashboards
Ratios
Green / red flag system
Presentation & structure
Layout and readability
Consistent structure
Attachments labelled as Decision or Information
Recommendations
Finance reporting

The annual financial statements should be included in the pack and be circulated before the meeting, not just listed on the agenda. This lets attendees come ready with questions. To get this started: Attach the Profit & Loss and Balance Sheet to the pack that goes out before the AGM. They already exist – this is about including them with the agenda.

Decision items

Each item that is needing a decision should be clearly marked. The agenda already signals item headings (confirming minutes, receiving reports, electing positions). Flagging these as decision items will help attendees prepare accordingly. A quick way to do this: Add a short word next to each item – 'Decision' or 'Information' – on the agenda template.

Supporting papers

For items where attendees need to decide something, a short background paper should be circulated with the agenda – what the issue is, the options, and what's recommended. One way to start: Pick the biggest decision on the next agenda and write a half-page paper for it. Build the habit from there.

Risk and compliance tracking

The board should adopt a practice of using a Risk Register as a simple way to see key risks and compliance obligations at each meeting. A practical first step: Build on the Risk Management Identification Template and possibly adapt the TWH template, and list your top five risks and key compliance deadlines in a single table, and include it in the board pack.

Tāhuhu | Direction The vision is real and genuinely guides decisions – the missing layer is a plan with measures, and a second income stream.
▾

The Tāhuhu is the ridgepole that elevates the kaupapa. This reflects the backbone of the strategy, to provide clear direction and a sense of priorities.

Reflects Mana Āheinga – mana that comes from deciding our own aspirations, and charting our own path forward. True kaupapa contribute to building the hope, aspirations, capabilities and empowerment of the collective, so that in turn they can elevate the mana of the kaupapa.

“Ko te pae tawhiti, whāia kia tata. Ko te pae tata, whakamaua kia tina.”
Seek out the distant horizon so that it may draw near. Hold fast to what is close, so that it endures.
Strong on the what – thin on the how, when and who.

The board checks its big decisions against the strategy. What it can't yet see is progress made, or the income streams to fund the journey.

Strengths
A clear 25-year vision with five focus areas▾
Purpose and community are well defined, the presentation is culturally grounded, and the five focus areas give the long horizon a workable shape.
The strategy is used, not shelved▾
Trustees describe linking significant decisions (e.g. investments, funding applications) back to the strategy as a regular check.
The approach to activating strategy is written down▾
Board ownership, an annual review rhythm, and having operational plans and budgets linked to the strategy are all described in the Manual.
Areas for attention
The next full strategy refresh is on the horizon with known ingredients▾
The strategy documents don't yet include a situational analysis or a stakeholder analysis. These are natural additions when the next full refresh comes. The vision itself is not expired.
⚑RF #6
No annual plan or visible measures for the strategyThe Manual references an annual operational plan holding each focus area's objectives and measures. That plan is not currently in place, so the AGM has been the only check-in on progress.
⚑RF #8
Most income rests on one lease that may not lastThe vineyard lease underpins the marae's income, and those close to the arrangement describe it as uneconomic for the lessee and expect it may wind down. No alternative income mechanism is clear.

Recommendations to improve your Tāhuhu

Secure the financial future – with a forward budget and a plan beyond the lease
⚑7⚑8Rec #8First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Shift the focus from looking backwards at historical spending to working to a budget for the year ahead, and a plan for income beyond the lease.

Context

The year-end financial statements are a strength, with income well broken out, a clear surplus, and every line compared to the prior year. But no forward budget was sighted, and the Manual's own Annual Budget Policy commits the board to preparing one and monitoring it monthly. A few governance-critical lines also aren't visible in the accounts – insurance, payments to people, a contingency fund.

Meanwhile the largest income stream (the vineyard lease) is under pressure, and those close to the arrangement describe it as uneconomic for the lessee and expect it may wind down. Ideas for the next income stream already exist in the kōrero – a solar array on the back land (with iwi-energy expertise close to the board and feasibility funding windows possible), grazing, and more bookings.

Practical ideas
  • Build a one-page forward budget off the current year's actuals, adding the lines currently invisible – insurance, payments to people, a named contingency.
  • Label the purpose of each bank account so that reserves and earmarked funds are visible.
  • Task one person to scope the solar feasibility option when the next funding window opens.
  • Add 'lease dependence' into the Risk Register and agree on how this will be tracked and mitigated.
Cost of inaction
  • The financial surplus erodes quietly without a plan to protect it.
  • The phone call about ending the lease arrives before the alternative income exists.
  • Feasibility-funding windows open and close unused.
Turn the vision into a trackable annual plan
⚑6Rec #9First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Embody the mana in the strategy by giving it motion – with a brief annual plan that sets out a few measures, so the board can see progress rather than just direction.

Context

The strategy itself is a genuine strength – a clear 25-year vision, five focus areas, and a board that habitually checks big decisions against it. The Manual's strategy scaffolding is strong, and points to an annual operational plan holding each focus area's objectives and measures.

That plan is actually not in place however (confirmed in the kōrero) – so the AGM has been the only check-in on progress. A recent strategy refresh means this is not about re-writing the strategy; it is about the thin layer between vision and action.

Practical ideas
  • For each of the five focus areas, agree one or two specific 12-month targets.
  • Have a short progress note on each of the focus areas as a regular agenda item at hui, and at the AGM.
  • Consider starting this before the AGM so that you've addressed it internally before it is asked about at the AGM. Then the practice will be in place when the new board start after the AGM.
Cost of inaction
  • Effort spreads thin because nothing says which few things matter this year.
  • The annual strategic review stays a conversation rather than an assessment.
  • The vision slowly loses its grip on day-to-day mahi.
Board Documents – Tāhuhu▾
Strategic Plan (25 Year Vision, read with the Manual's strategy sections) – 🟠 Rich in identity and direction; light in visible measurement
15102
▾
Hau Ariki's strategy is carried across two documents, and read together they tell a strong story. The 25-year vision map paints a clear, long-horizon picture of the physical marae, grounded in te reo and the kaupapa of Tikanga, Te Reo and Mātauranga. The Governance Manual adds the written scaffolding to translate it into operational guidance – five strategic focus areas, a defined purpose and community, and a governance rhythm in which the Board owns the strategy, reviews it annually, and links operational plans and budgets to it. The main opportunity is to make the specific objectives and measures visible to the board, as these currently sit in the annual operational plan (which isn't evident) rather than in the strategy itself.
15 items complete – A clear 25-year vision, five strategic focus areas, a defined purpose and community, and a full governance rhythm – Board ownership, annual review, and operational plans and budgets linked to strategy.
10 items partial – The specific objectives, actions and measures sit in the annual operational plan, which wasn't provided – so SMART targets and KPIs aren't yet visible in the strategy itself.
2 gaps – A formal situational analysis (SWOT or environmental scan) and the written Vision and Mission statement wording are not evident in the documents reviewed.
Strategic plan components
Te Pae Tawhiti – long-term vision expressed
Te Aronga Nui – purpose and community defined
Ngā Kaupapa – values and kaupapa Māori expressed
Ngā Whāinga Matua – five strategic focus areas established
Ngā Wero Matua – barriers and risks identified
Ngā Putanga – key outcomes in SMART format
Ngā Inenga Angitu – success measures and KPIs
Strategic alignment
Activities clearly serve the marae community
Clear identification of who the marae serves
Activities are high-impact for the marae
Stated priorities match the projects shown
Avoids legacy or sunk-cost thinking
Strategic framework structures the strategy
Accountability and ownership assigned
Monitoring and review process described
Strategy is a living tool with a review rhythm
Goals express end outcomes rather than means
Goals are specific and actionable
Goals are focused and manageable in number
Goals are realistic given capacity
Plan acknowledges constraints and risks
Presentation & structure
Layout and readability
Culturally grounded presentation
Historical and organisational context
Consistent structure across focus areas
Stakeholder analysis
SWOT or environmental scan
Annual Operational Plan Not yet reviewed
Maihi | Performance Delivery is real and the accounts are strong – the gaps are a forward budget and protected governance time.
▾

Maihi represent the bargeboards of the whare – that connect the Tāhuhu to the Tūāpapa as the arms that turn your strategy into action. This represents the management systems, the operational rhythms and having the right people.

Reflects Mana Whakahaere – mana that comes from turning leadership decisions into disciplined action to ensure the performance of a kaupapa.

“Ko te Amorangi ki mua, ko te Hāpai ō ki muri. Te tūturutanga mahi pono o te Māori mana motuhake.”
Guided by visionary leadership and well provisioned. This is what upholds true Māori self-determination.
Western lens
Performance is defined by outputs, throughputs and financial ratios – what can be counted and compared.
Kaupapa Māori lens
Performance is impact for whānau and the community the marae serves – moments that matter, mauri, and the long arc of intergenerational outcome.
Operationally this marae performs – the oversight layer looks backwards.

A strong surplus and clean statements deserve a forward plan to match.

Strengths
A strong financial record, clearly reported▾
Income is broken out into streams, a clear surplus showing for the year, every line compared to the prior year, and capital additions and depreciation visible on the Balance Sheet.
Financial controls are in place▾
Two-signatory banking, delegated financial authorities with clear thresholds, and complete expenditure and receipting rules – among the strongest sections of the Manual.
A track record of getting funded and delivering▾
Solar and batteries, fire sprinklers, water catchment, facility upgrades and a Community Resilience Centre role – grants won and projects landed.
Areas for attention
The governance–operations line is carried by too few hands▾
The board is also the operating team. The Marae Committee architecture exists – the gap is capacity and an agreed split of what belongs where. This is a structural reality to design around, rather than a failing.
⚑RF #7
No forward budget to steer byThe bank statements show what happened, but not what's planned – and governance-critical lines such as insurance, payments to people and a contingency budget aren't visible. The Manual's own Annual Budget Policy commits the board to preparing and monitoring one.

Recommendations to improve your Maihi

Stand up a part-time paid administrator
Rec #10First 90 days
▾
Purpose

Break the volunteer capacity spiral by resourcing some key administrative basics – so trustees can govern, and the mahi still gets done.

Context

Every voice in this engagement backed some form of paid part-time administration, and the board has already discussed it once without landing it. The shape it could take seems consistent – a contractor for around a day a week, sorting marae bookings, organising hui and events, document tidy-up, funding applications and follow-through. The kōrero was clear it needs to be someone who knows how to get things done and has community connections beyond the current group – not just a minute-taker.

The finances suggest the role is affordable – especially if it drives bookings income and funding applications that cover part of its own cost.

Practical ideas
  • Finalise the position description the board has already discussed and attach a number of hours to it.
  • Agree who will complete and submit the funding application for the administrator role, and by when.
  • Build "drives bookings and income" into the role so it works toward covering its own cost.
  • Prefer a local appointment with strong community connections – ideally from one of the local Māori whānau, an idea raised in the kōrero.
Cost of inaction
  • The same few volunteers keep carrying everything, and burn out.
  • Good ideas keep lapsing in silence because no one has the hours to carry them.
  • The marae's bookings and funding potential stays untapped.
Protect governance time – clarify the governance–operations line
Rec #11First 6 months
▾
Purpose

Let a small board spend its scarce hui time actually governing – while the operational mahi can still get done, visibly and sustainably.

Context

This board also takes on much of its own operations. There are strong signals that the line between governance and operational mahi has blurred – it was named from every seat, and the trustees closest to it are candid that they lean into operations because the work has to get done.

The Marae Committee exists and meets monthly – so the architecture is there. The gap is enough capacity in the Committee to get it all done, and an agreed split of what actions belong where. The paid administrator role and a Marae Committee make this practical.

Practical ideas
  • Split each board agenda into a governance section and a brief operational update.
  • Agree which operational decisions the Marae Committee simply gets on with between hui.
  • Recruit a Chair for the Marae Committee to run the monthly operational hui.
  • Revisit the split six months after the administrator starts – it should feel noticeably lighter.
Cost of inaction
  • Governance keeps getting squeezed by hands-on mahi, so the forward-looking work never starts.
  • The trustee role keeps looking like unpaid operations work, which is exactly what makes recruitment hard.
  • Oversight vs doing blur together into burnout.
Board Documents – Maihi▾
Annual Financial Statements (FY to 31 March 2025) – 🟠 A strong record of what happened; not yet a plan for what's ahead
1524
▾
These are the Trust's year-end financial statements – a Profit & Loss, a Balance Sheet, and an AGM financial report with variance commentary. They report clearly what actually happened in the year to 31 March 2025. As a record of income and spending, the package is strong: income is well broken out, the net surplus is clear, and every line is compared against the prior year. The main opportunities are to show a few governance-critical costs that aren't currently visible – insurance, payments to people, and a contingency buffer. Ideally this is paired with a forward-looking budget for the year ahead that the board can track against.
15 items complete – Income detail, the net position, prior-year comparison, capital additions, and depreciation are all clearly visible.
2 items partial – Forward income certainty and reserve fund contributions are signalled but not separately set out.
4 gaps – Insurance, payments to people, AGM costs, and a contingency buffer are not visible as separate lines.
Income and revenue
Income broken into distinct revenue streams (interest, koha, lease, marae hire, rent, grants)
Revenue diversification visible across trading income and grants
Grant income broken down by source and purpose in the commentary
Income certainty or renewal status for the year ahead
Operating expenditure
Power and utilities
Repairs and maintenance, with commentary on major items
Professional services (consulting & accounting; legal & compliance)
Supplies and consumables
Kai and manaakitanga
Rates
Personnel or payments to people
Insurance expense line
AGM expenses as a separate line
Contingency line
Capital and reserves
Capital additions to infrastructure visible on the balance sheet
Depreciation shown as a distinct line
Contribution to a named reserve or savings fund
Net position and historical context
Net surplus clearly shown ($112,604 for 2025)
Comparison with previous year's actuals
Variance commentary against the prior year
Balance sheet showing assets, equity, and retained earnings
Recommendations
A forward budget alongside the actuals

These statements show the board what happened last year. They should sit alongside a forward-looking budget for the year ahead, so the board can see the plan and then track actuals against it through the year. To get this started: Take this year's actuals as the starting point and build a simple one-page budget for the coming year – expected income by source, expected costs by line, and the surplus or deficit that results.

Insurance visibility

Insurance is a governance-critical cost, and the board should be able to see it as its own line. Right now it isn't visible anywhere in the statements. One way to do this: Confirm where the marae's building and public liability cover sits, and add it as a named line in both the accounts and any future budget.

Payments to people

If the marae pays anyone – wages, contractor fees, or koha for services – the board should be able to see it. No payments-to-people line is visible at the moment. A practical first step: Check whether any such payments are made and, if so, give them their own line.

A contingency buffer

A named contingency line gives the board confidence that there's room for unexpected costs – an urgent repair or a funding shortfall. This is most useful once a forward budget is in place. One way to do this: Set aside a small fixed amount or percentage as a named contingency line in next year's budget.

Reserve and earmarked funds

The trust holds several separate bank accounts. If any of these are intended as reserves, labelling them as such would help the board see what is set aside and why. A practical first step: Note the purpose of each account on the Balance Sheet, so the board can differentiate working cash from the earmarked or reserved funds.

Forward Budget (year ahead) Not yet reviewed
Pou Tuarongo | Future-proofing Board are naming the risks themselves – the work is in the continuity side such as the next board rotation, insurance, and the on-site heart of the marae.
▾
